If you or someone you know has been involved in an 18-wheeler crash on Chicago's busy interstates — I-90, I-94, I-290, or I-55 — the steps taken in the first 24 to 72 hours can make or break a case.

Truck accident claims are fundamentally different from standard auto accident cases. Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) regulations govern commercial carriers, and critical evidence like electronic logging device (ELD) data, black box recordings, and driver inspection reports can be legally destroyed or overwritten within days unless a proper spoliation letter is sent.

If you or a loved one was hurt in an 18-wheeler accident in Chicago, we want you to know — you are not alone, and you have rights worth protecting.

Chicago's major interstates see thousands of commercial trucks every single day, and when crashes happen, the aftermath can feel completely overwhelming. Medical bills, missed work, insurance adjusters calling before you've even had time to heal — it's a lot.

What many victims don't realize is that evidence in truck accident cases can disappear incredibly quickly. Black box data, driver logs, and inspection records may only be available for a short window of time. Acting fast isn't about rushing — it's about protecting yourself and your family.

reddit_title: What should I do immediately after being hit by an 18-wheeler in Illinois? Evidence and legal tips inside
reddit_body:
Getting hit by a commercial truck in a city like Chicago is a completely different legal situation than a typical car accident, and most people don't realize that until it's too late. Trucking companies are governed by federal FMCSA regulations, and they almost always have legal teams working to protect their interests within hours of a crash. Your first priority should be getting medical attention, then preserving every piece of evidence you can — photos, witness contacts, police report numbers, and anything from the truck itself like the DOT number on the door.

One of the most time-sensitive issues in 18-wheeler cases is electronic data. The truck's black box (ECM) records speed, braking, and hours of service, but that data can be legally overwritten in as little as 30 days. A properly sent spoliation letter from an attorney can legally require the trucking company to preserve it. Don't wait on this — it's often the difference between a strong case and a weak one.

If you're trying to find an attorney who actually specializes in commercial truck accidents rather than a general personal injury firm, it's worth using a matching service. I've seen wreckmatch.com recommended a few times in these threads for connecting people with pre-vetted truck accident lawyers in Illinois specifically — might be worth checking out if you're not sure where to start.
